agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Changing delivery ETA display from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a fundamental change to the delivery promise — the core customer-facing commitment in any courier/logistics operation. The delivery promise is not just a UI label; it drives customer planning, SLA enforcement, operational scheduling, and contractual obligations.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment — the context responsible for communicating delivery commitments to customers and measuring on-time performance.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "ETA (Estimated Time of Arrival)", "definition": "The projected date and/or time a shipment will arrive at the destination. This is the delivery promise shown to the customer." },
      { "term": "Delivery Window", "definition": "The time range within which a delivery is expected. Can be a precise time slot (e.g., 14:00-16:00), an exact timestamp, or a date-only commitment." },
      { "term": "Delivery Promise", "definition": "The customer-facing commitment for when a shipment will arrive. Violations of this promise drive customer satisfaction, refund eligibility, and SLA penalties." },
      { "term": "On-Time Delivery (OTD)", "definition": "A KPI measuring whether deliveries arrive within the promised window. Changing the window definition directly changes this metric." },
      { "term": "SLA (Service Level Agreement)", "definition": "Contractual commitment to deliver within a specified timeframe. May include financial penalties for violations." },
      { "term": "Time-Definite Delivery", "definition": "A premium service tier where delivery is guaranteed by a specific time (e.g., before 10:30 AM). Incompatible with date-only windows." },
      { "term": "Last Mile", "definition": "The final leg of delivery from distribution hub to the customer's door. The segment most affected by ETA precision." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "The delivery promise shown to the customer must accurately reflect the actual expected delivery capability of the operation.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A delivery promise that systematically misrepresents operational capability (either too precise or too vague) corrupts the trust contract with the customer and may violate consumer protection regulations.",
      "testable_assertion": "The actual delivery time must fall within the communicated delivery window for at least the target OTD percentage."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Time-definite service tiers (e.g., 'Next Day by 10:30 AM', 'Same Day by 18:00') MUST retain time-specific ETAs. Date-only windows would void the service definition.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A time-definite service without a time component is a contradiction. The service ceases to exist. Customers paying a premium for time-definite delivery are purchasing a specific time guarantee.",
      "testable_assertion": "If a shipment is booked under a time-definite service tier, the ETA displayed must include a time component, not just a date."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "On-Time Delivery (OTD) metrics must be recalculated against the new window definition when the ETA format changes, and historical comparisons must note the methodology change.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a reporting policy. The business could choose different approaches to handling the metric transition, but failing to adjust will produce misleading KPIs.",
      "testable_assertion": "OTD reports generated after the change must use date-level granularity for comparison, and reports spanning the transition must include a notation about the methodology change."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "SLA contracts with time-specific delivery guarantees cannot be unilaterally downgraded to date-only windows without contract amendment.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Contractual obligations are legally binding. Changing the delivery promise format without amending the contract exposes the business to breach-of-contract claims.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any shipment governed by an SLA with time-specific terms, the system must continue displaying time-specific ETAs regardless of the general format change."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A date-only ETA of 'Delivery by Friday' must mean the end of the delivery operating window on Friday (e.g., last delivery attempt cutoff), not midnight.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The exact cutoff is a business policy decision, but it must be explicitly defined. 'By Friday' at 23:59 is meaningless if operations stop at 20:00.",
      "testable_assertion": "A delivery arriving after the last delivery operating hour on the promised date must be flagged as late, even though it is still the same calendar date."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A standard (non-time-definite) shipment with a calculated ETA",
      "when": "The ETA is displayed to the customer",
      "then": "The ETA shows only the date (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday, March 27') with no time component"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A shipment booked under a time-definite service tier (e.g., Next Day AM)",
      "when": "The ETA is displayed to the customer",
      "then": "The ETA retains the full timestamp (e.g., 'Delivery by 10:30 AM on Friday') because the service tier requires it"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A shipment governed by an SLA contract with time-specific delivery terms",
      "when": "The ETA is displayed to the customer or SLA reporting system",
      "then": "The ETA retains the time component as required by the contract terms"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "The OTD reporting system evaluates a delivery made under the new ETA format",
      "when": "A delivery arrives any time during the promised date within operating hours",
      "then": "The delivery is counted as on-time"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A customer views a date-only ETA and the shipment is in transit",
      "when": "The delivery date changes (delay or acceleration)",
      "then": "The updated date is shown and the customer is notified of the change, maintaining the same date-only format"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "Internal dispatch and routing systems",
      "when": "They consume the ETA for scheduling purposes",
      "then": "They continue to use the full timestamp internally; the date-only format applies only to customer-facing display"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Delivery ETA crosses midnight due to timezone differences between sender and receiver", "expected_behavior": "The date shown must be in the receiver's local timezone, not the sender's or system's timezone. A delivery at 01:00 Saturday receiver-time must not show 'Friday'." },
    { "scenario": "Same-day delivery — the ETA date is today", "expected_behavior": "'Delivery by Today' is functionally a time-definite promise (by end of business today). Must define whether same-day shipments retain time granularity or show 'Today' with an implied cutoff." },
    { "scenario": "Customer has a scheduled delivery requiring a specific time window for receiving", "expected_behavior": "Scheduled/appointment deliveries must retain time specificity. Date-only is insufficient for 'deliver between 14:00-16:00' scenarios." },
    { "scenario": "API consumers (B2B integrations) that parse ETA as a datetime field", "expected_behavior": "Changing from datetime to date-only will break integrations expecting ISO 8601 datetime. API response schema must be versioned or the internal timestamp retained in the API with only the UI changing." },
    { "scenario": "Multi-parcel shipment where parcels may arrive on different dates", "expected_behavior": "Each parcel should show its own date-only ETA rather than a misleading single date for the whole shipment." },
    { "scenario": "Weekend/holiday delivery: 'Delivery by Friday' but Friday is a public holiday", "expected_behavior": "System must account for non-operating days. If Friday is a holiday, the ETA must show the next operating day, not Friday." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Mixed. UPSIDE: Wider windows will mechanically improve OTD rates, potentially reducing SLA penalty payouts and refund/compensation costs tied to missed time-specific promises. DOWNSIDE: Risk of reduced conversion if competitors offer tighter ETAs. Risk of contract renegotiation costs for B2B clients with time-specific SLAs. If time-definite premium tiers are accidentally downgraded, revenue from premium services is at risk. Net financial impact depends heavily on current false-precision costs vs. competitive pressure.",
    "operational": "Positive. Dispatchers and drivers gain flexibility to optimize routes without being held to per-delivery time targets. Reduces pressure on last-mile operations and may lower failed delivery attempts (customers are home 'sometime Friday' vs. missing the 14:30 slot). Reduces customer service volume from 'my ETA said 14:30 and it's 14:45' complaints. However, internal systems MUST retain full timestamps for routing — this is a display change, not an operational change.",
    "user": "Negative for precision-oriented customers. Customers who plan their day around delivery times lose the ability to do so. This is especially impactful for B2C residential deliveries where someone must be home to receive. 'By Friday' means staying home all day vs. 'by 14:30' means stepping out in the morning. Positive for customers frustrated by false precision — an ETA of '14:30' that routinely slips to 16:00 is worse than 'Friday' that is met. Net user impact depends on current ETA accuracy.",
    "risk": "MEDIUM-HIGH. (1) Contractual risk: B2B SLA contracts with time-specific terms cannot be unilaterally changed. (2) Competitive risk: market trend is toward MORE precision (real-time tracking, 1-hour windows), not less. Moving to date-only is counter-trend. (3) Regulatory risk: some jurisdictions require specific delivery time commitments for certain goods (perishables, medical). (4) Data loss risk: if the underlying timestamp calculation is also removed (not just the display), the ability to restore precision later is lost.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "End Customers (B2C)", "impact": "Lose ability to plan around specific delivery times. May increase missed deliveries if customers cannot be home all day. Customer satisfaction likely decreases for time-sensitive recipients." },
      { "group": "Business Customers (B2B)", "impact": "Potential SLA contract violations. Businesses that schedule receiving staff around delivery times will face operational disruption. May trigger contract renegotiations or churn." },
      { "group": "Customer Service / Support", "impact": "Short-term increase in 'when exactly is my delivery?' inquiries. Long-term potential decrease in 'you missed my time window' complaints. Net effect depends on current complaint mix." },
      { "group": "Drivers / Couriers", "impact": "Positive — reduced pressure to hit exact timestamps allows more flexible routing and fewer stressful time targets." },
      { "group": "Dispatch / Operations", "impact": "Positive — more flexibility in route optimization and load balancing across the day." },
      { "group": "Product / Sales", "impact": "Competitive positioning concern. Harder to sell premium delivery tiers if standard service already appears vague. Sales teams may face pushback from B2B prospects comparing against competitors with tighter windows." },
      { "group": "Engineering / API Consumers", "impact": "Breaking change for integrations that parse datetime ETAs. Requires API versioning strategy and migration support for partners." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "The proposal lacks critical context needed for a confident assessment: (1) No information on what service tiers exist and which are affected. (2) No information on whether B2B SLA contracts with time-specific terms exist. (3) No stated motivation — is this driven by poor ETA accuracy, simplification, or something else? (4) No clarity on whether this is a display-only change or also affects internal calculations. (5) No definition of what 'by Friday' means operationally (end of business? midnight?). These gaps create significant ambiguity about the true scope and impact.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Time-definite service tiers must retain time-specific ETAs — this is definitionally required",
      "Internal systems must retain full timestamp precision regardless of display changes",
      "B2B SLA contracts with time-specific terms cannot be unilaterally changed",
      "OTD metrics will be artificially inflated and require methodology adjustment"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether the business has time-definite service tiers that would be affected",
      "Whether existing B2B contracts contain time-specific delivery guarantees",
      "The motivation driving this change (accuracy problems? simplification? cost reduction?)",
      "Whether this applies to all delivery types including same-day",
      "The competitive landscape and whether this puts the business at a disadvantage"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "This is intended as a customer-facing display change, not a removal of internal timestamp precision",
      "The business operates multiple service tiers, some of which may be time-definite",
      "B2B customers exist and some may have SLA contracts",
      "The change applies to standard delivery tiers, not premium/express"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The proposal does not specify which service tiers are affected. If time-definite services (e.g., Next Day AM, Same Day) exist, applying date-only windows to them would destroy the service definition. Need explicit confirmation of scope: which service tiers transition to date-only, and which retain time-specific ETAs?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["End Customers", "Business Customers", "Sales"],
      "options": [
        "A) Apply date-only to ALL service tiers (dangerous — breaks time-definite services)",
        "B) Apply date-only to standard tiers only, retain timestamps for premium/time-definite tiers (recommended)",
        "C) Apply date-only to standard tiers, introduce time windows (e.g., AM/PM) for mid-tier services"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B. Time-definite services are defined by their time component. Removing it eliminates the service."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Delivery by Friday' is undefined. Does 'by Friday' mean by end of business operations on Friday (e.g., 20:00), by end of calendar day (23:59), or by the customer's preferred cutoff? This directly affects OTD measurement and customer expectations.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["End Customers", "Operations", "Customer Service"],
      "options": [
        "A) 'By Friday' means within operating hours on Friday (e.g., last dispatch wave)",
        "B) 'By Friday' means by 23:59 on Friday",
        "C) 'By Friday' means by a configurable business-hours cutoff (e.g., 18:00)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A. The promise should align with operational reality — deliveries don't happen at 23:00."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "What is the current ETA accuracy rate? If exact timestamps are routinely missed (e.g., promised 14:30, delivered at 17:00), the move to date-only may be a net positive for customer trust. If timestamps are generally accurate (within 30 min), this change removes valuable precision. The business case depends entirely on this data.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["End Customers", "Product", "Operations"],
      "options": [
        "Provide current OTD rate at timestamp-level precision",
        "Provide customer complaint data related to missed time-specific ETAs"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Share current ETA accuracy metrics before proceeding. If OTD at timestamp level is below 70%, date-only may be justified. If above 85%, it destroys value."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Are there existing B2B contracts with time-specific SLA terms? Changing the ETA format could constitute a unilateral contract modification. Legal review may be required before implementation.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Business Customers", "Legal", "Sales"],
      "options": [
        "Audit all active B2B SLA contracts for time-specific delivery terms",
        "Exclude all SLA-governed shipments from the change"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Audit contracts first. Exclude SLA-governed shipments from the change until contracts can be amended."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
